medications.indication_rationale: Diuretics are the mainstay for cardiogenic pulmonary oedema but are not indicated and will not help non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema/ARDS, where the problem is increased capillary permeability rather than volume overload; recommending this would reflect a diagnostic error and delay appropriate care.
contraindications_in_this_case: No evidence of volume overload; underlying process is inflammatory/permeability-driven, not hydrostatic

medications.drug_name: home oxygen or any device the patient/carer might have available
medications.indication_rationale: If a home oxygen concentrator or similar is already available and prescribed for another reason, using it while awaiting the ambulance is reasonable, but must not delay or replace the emergency call.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Minimise talking and conserve energy; let the carer communicate with emergency services on her behalf.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces additional oxygen demand from speech and physical effort in a severely hypoxaemic, exhausted patient.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Remain resting quietly in whatever position is most comfortable (positioning will not reverse this process, unlike in heart failure) while awaiting the ambulance.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Since repositioning does not relieve non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, the priority is minimising exertion rather than achieving a specific position.

patient_education_points.point: Your lungs appear to be inflamed and leaking fluid following the choking episode a day and a half ago — this is not a heart problem, and standard heart medicines like water tablets won't help. You need specialised breathing support at the hospital.

Please call 000 for an ambulance right away and mention there was a recent choking/aspiration event with worsening breathing and very low oxygen levels — this may need an intensive care paramedic team with advanced breathing equipment.
Try not to talk more than necessary — let your carer speak to the 000 operator and paramedics for you so you can save your energy for breathing.
If she becomes unresponsive or stops breathing, lay her flat on the floor and begin CPR immediately, following the 000 dispatcher's instructions.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the intensive care and respiratory team after treatment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge (if survives the acute episode): respiratory rehabilitation, swallowing assessment to prevent further aspiration events, review of the antibiotic course completed
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Unresponsiveness or breathing stopping — begin CPR immediately per dispatcher instructions.

must_include_items: Call 000 immediately, specifying recent aspiration event and severe/refractory hypoxaemia so an intensive-care-capable team is dispatched
Explicit statement that this is a lung problem (likely ARDS from aspiration), NOT primarily a heart problem
Instruction to minimise talking/exertion to conserve oxygen
Safety-netting with CPR instructions if the patient becomes unresponsive or stops breathing
acceptable_alternatives: Using already-available home oxygen equipment while awaiting the ambulance is acceptable as a bridging measure, but must not delay or replace the 000 call
errors_of_omission: No recognition that this is likely non-cardiac (ARDS) rather than heart failure
No instruction to conserve energy/minimise talking
errors_of_commission: Recommending diuretics or other heart-failure-directed treatment as the primary management
Framing this as simple heart failure and suggesting upright positioning alone will resolve it
Continuing extended history-taking with a severely fatigued patient instead of escalating promptly
Recommending a routine GP or respiratory clinic appointment instead of emergency escalation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include (1) immediate 000 call specifying the aspiration/refractory-hypoxaemia context AND (2) correct non-cardiac framing is an automatic FAIL. This is a T5 case with profound, refractory hypoxaemia and a high likelihood of impending respiratory failure requiring intubation.
